name: B2B SaaS cold intro to ops/RevOps
icp: Head of RevOps, Sales Ops, or BizOps at a 50–500-person B2B SaaS
purpose: Land a 20-min call to walk through the pain and see if the product fits
subjectLines: Quick one for the {{company}} ops team
Saw the {{specificObservation}} — curious how it's going
body:
Hi {{firstName}},

Noticed {{specificObservation}}. Usually when a team gets to that stage, {{painPoint}} starts eating an unreasonable amount of the ops team's week.

We built {{productName}} to fix that one specific thing. {{customerExampleA}} cut their {{metric}} from {{beforeMetric}} to {{afterMetric}} in {{timeframe}}, with no extra headcount.

Not trying to replace your stack. It plugs into {{integrationStack}} and runs alongside what you already have.

If the timing's right, I can show you the {{customerExampleA}} setup in 20 minutes — happy to send the recording instead if a live call is too much.

Worth a conversation?

{{senderFirstName}}

variables.note: A signal: hiring a RevOps role, recent funding, a podcast appearance, a tool migration. Avoid generic 'congrats on the growth.'
The pain in their language. e.g. 'lead routing latency', 'pipeline hygiene', 'attribution gaps'.
Use only if you have permission. Otherwise drop the case-study sentence and rewrite around the product.
The metric that moved. e.g. 'lead-to-meeting conversion', 'time-to-first-touch'.
The 1–2 tools they almost certainly already use. e.g. 'Salesforce + Outreach'.

notes: RevOps people are skeptical of cold email by trade — they read these for a living. The win here is being concrete: name the pain, name the metric, name the tools you integrate with, and offer a recording instead of a call. Drop the case-study paragraph entirely if you can't substantiate it; a vague claim is worse than no claim.
